title: Stop payment vs insufficient funds - does it change the case
p1: A cheque bounced. But does the reason behind it really matter? Many people assume that a stop payment instruction is completely different from insufficient funds when it comes to legal consequences.
p2: The surprising part: both situations can still lead to the same legal issue under cheque bounce laws. A stop payment does not automatically protect the person who issued the cheque. If the cheque was issued towards a genuine payment obligation and other conditions are met, the case may still proceed.
p3: The difference lies in the reason for dishonour. Insufficient funds usually means the account could not cover the cheque amount. Stop payment means the account holder instructed the bank not to honour it. But the court may look beyond the bank's reason and examine why the cheque was issued in the first place. The practical lesson: stopping a cheque is not a guaranteed escape route from liability.
conclusion: Have you ever thought that a stop payment instruction could still result in a cheque bounce case? What is your view on this distinction?
